subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Taylor / Maclaurin Series
stimulus: The function $f$ has a Taylor series about $x = 1$ that converges to $f(x)$ for all $x$ in the interval of convergence. It is known that $f(1) = 1$, $f'(1) = -\dfrac{1}{2}$, and the $n$th derivative of $f$ at $x = 1$ is given by $f^{(n)}(1) = \dfrac{(-1)^n (n-1)!}{2^n}$ for $n \geq 2$.

parts.prompt: Write the first four nonzero terms and the general term of the Taylor series for $f$ about $x = 1$.
parts.scoring_notes: The Taylor series is $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{f^{(n)}(1)}{n!}(x-1)^n$. For $n \geq 2$: $\dfrac{f^{(n)}(1)}{n!} = \dfrac{(-1)^n (n-1)!}{2^n \cdot n!} = \dfrac{(-1)^n}{n \cdot 2^n}$. For $n=1$: coefficient $= f'(1) = -\dfrac{1}{2}$. For $n=0$: $f(1)=1$.

parts.prompt: The Taylor series for $f$ about $x = 1$ has a radius of convergence of $2$. Find the interval of convergence. Show the work that leads to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Radius of convergence $= 2$ means $|x - 1| < 2$, so $-1 < x < 3$. Check the endpoints $x = -1$ and $x = 3$ for convergence.

required_elements: At $x = -1$: series becomes $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{n \cdot 2^n}(-2)^n = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n \cdot (-1)^n \cdot 2^n}{n \cdot 2^n} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n}$, which diverges (harmonic series). At $x = 3$: series becomes $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{n}$, which converges by the Alternating Series Test.

parts.prompt: The Taylor series for $f$ about $x = 1$ can be used to represent $f(1.2)$ as an alternating series. Use the first three nonzero terms of the alternating series to approximate $f(1.2)$.
parts.scoring_notes: Substitute $x = 1.2$, so $x - 1 = 0.2$, into the first three nonzero terms.

scoring_points.description: Correct approximation using first three terms with $x - 1 = 0.2$

parts.prompt: Show that the approximation found in part (c) is within $0.001$ of the exact value of $f(1.2)$.
parts.scoring_notes: Since the series is alternating and the terms are decreasing in absolute value, the error is bounded by the absolute value of the first omitted term.

scoring_points.description: States and applies the Alternating Series Estimation Theorem (error $\leq |a_{n+1}|$)

required_elements: By the Alternating Series Estimation Theorem, the error is at most the absolute value of the first omitted term

scoring_points.description: Correctly computes the first omitted term and shows it is less than $0.001$
